name: Razvan Rotaru
workplace: Student @ UAIC, Faculty of Computer Science (year 3)
goals:
He wants to interact with the house by just looking at things. Instead of pulling out his phone to check the thermostat, he wants to glance at it and see the temperature floating in the air right next to it, then adjust it with a pinch gesture without touching anything.
He wants to move through the house naturally and have the system react to his presence. Walking past the living room speaker and swiping his hand to send the music to his bedroom speaker feels way more satisfying to him than navigating a playlist screen.
He wants the AR interface to feel like it belongs in the real world, not on top of it. Translucent panels, subtle animations, and anchored overlays that stay stuck to the physical objects they represent, not floating menus that block his view of the room.
frustrations:
It drives him crazy when he has to look away from what he's doing just to check something on a screen. If he's fixing something in the kitchen and needs to check the oven temperature, taking off his glasses to unlock his phone completely kills his flow.
He hates heavy, opaque UI that covers his field of view. If the AR overlay blocks more of the room than it reveals, the whole point is lost. He wants to see the house and the data at the same time, not one or the other.
He gets frustrated when spatial interactions feel unresponsive or imprecise. If he pinches to dim a light and nothing happens for half a second, it breaks the immersion entirely and makes the whole experience feel like a gimmick.
scenarios: Razvan is sketching at his desk when he notices the room feels too warm. Without stopping what he's doing, he glances toward the thermostat on the wall, and a small floating panel appears next to it showing the current temperature. He pinches the air and drags it down slightly to lower it by two degrees, then looks back at his sketchbook. The panel fades away on its own after a few seconds.
otherDetails: His driving force is immersion. He sees the AR interface not as a tool but as an extension of the space itself. For him, the best interaction is one that feels like magic, invisible enough that it doesn't interrupt real life, but powerful enough that he'd never want to go back to a regular app.
